title: Near Dark
tech: Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [5294 400T period-probable] · Arriflex [body unverified] · Arriflex/Zeiss primes [set unverified] · 35mm spherical 1.85:1 · CFI lab · Technicolor prints
writeup: Near Dark is a 1987 vampire-western photographed by Adam Greenberg in spherical 35mm — a choice that roots its mythology in the flat American West rather than the elongated shadows of European gothic. Greenberg's approach was naturalistic-looking but heavily controlled: light shaped to serve character as mythic figure, not environment as mood machine. The result is a specific, identifiable grammar — deep velvety blacks that photochemically refuse shadow detail, hazy magic-hour burnouts where dawn functions as a lethal weapon, a dusty desaturated earth-tone palette punctuated only by neon, fire, and blood. A blue-night / amber-dawn polarity operates as emotional and narrative logic throughout. Bigelow stripped every gothic marker from the frame; what remains looks like a revisionist western and reads like a romance built on danger.

presets.name: Open Highway Night
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Wide exterior night, Oklahoma flatland two-lane blacktop, headlights as the only practical light source cutting through dust haze, deep velvety black sky with no atmospheric glow or city spill, road surface catching amber-orange headlight falloff, surrounding land consumed by photochemical darkness, grain heavy in shadow zones, horizon line held low, anti-gothic open American emptiness, no moonlight fill, no recoverable shadow detail, slight halation around headlight sources from fast stock, naturalistic but heavily shaped light that refuses to romanticize the dark.
presets.neg: digital clean blacks, moonlit blue fill, HDR shadow recovery, urban glow, gothic atmosphere, fog machine haze, studio setup feel, glamour lighting, star-field detail, modern color grade, neon-noir glow, horror atmosphere

presets.name: Bar Massacre
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Interior roadhouse bar, practical neon signage as primary color source — red and blue-green neon casting hard isolated pools across floor and faces, warm tungsten practicals in background creating amber fills, deep shadow zones between light pools with no detail recovery, fire and flame erupting as punctuation in the controlled palette, figures silhouetted or half-lit by neon cross-light, smashed glass catching point-source specular reflections, blood reading near-black against dusty floor, heavy grain in underexposed areas, smoke threading through neon beams, photochemical neon bloom without digital glow, neon saturation cutting through an otherwise desaturated interior.
presets.neg: clean studio lighting, modern horror color grade, gothic chandeliers, even ambient fill, HDR neon bloom, digital glow effects, contemporary bar design, glamour hero lighting, clean shadow separation, polished surfaces

presets.name: Dawn Scramble
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Magic hour exterior dawn, flat Oklahoma highway, daylight arriving as a hostile overexposed wall of amber-to-white sky, foreground still in blue-black transition as night recedes, characters in frantic silhouette against the blinding horizon burn, fast tungsten stock blown into irrecoverable highlight clipping at the sun angle, blue lingering in the near-ground shadow zone, color polarity shift from cold night to burning orange-white dawn operating as literal threat in-frame, dusty road surface catching first horizontal light, grain exaggerated in the transition zone between deep shadow and blown highlight, speed and danger encoded in the exposure choice.
presets.neg: golden hour beauty lighting, HDR sky detail recovery, romantic sunrise, landscape photography, controlled exposure, digital tone mapping, soft warmth, lush color palette, slow tranquil dawn, travel photography

presets.name: Motel Room Night
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Interior motel room night, single practical bedside lamp as key casting warm tungsten amber on nearest surfaces, blue-black darkness filling the room beyond lamp range, window suggesting exterior night as a secondary cool blue source, worn American motel materials — polyester bedspread, water-stained ceiling, thin curtain — catching light as texture not glamour, figures in intimate proximity with split-light: one side warm amber, one side lost in deep shadow, grain prominent in blue-darkness areas away from the lamp, no fill card, no ambient recovery, photochemical blacks eating the room edges, refuge that is also a trap.
presets.neg: hotel photography lighting, clean ambient, beauty key, romantic candlelight, HDR interior, modern color grade, production design showcase, lifestyle photography, moody editorial glamour, soft romantic fill

presets.name: The Stalk
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Exterior night, figure moving through the edge of a rural American non-place — parking lot perimeter, gas station forecourt, roadside drainage ditch — deep blue overall color temperature from fast tungsten stock in near-moonless environment, single distant practical (fluorescent forecourt, lit window) providing directionality without fill, figure partially dissolved into surrounding darkness with only face or raised hands reading against the black, grain heavy in shadow zones, low to mid-angle framing that keeps the figure as part of the darkness rather than against it, wide foreground black emphasizing rural isolation, predator logic encoded in the exposure choice.
presets.neg: backlit hero reveal, blue moonlight beauty, clear ambient fill, theatrical monster lighting, dramatic silhouette, lens flare, gothic shadows, modern color grade, high-contrast fashion noir, horror jump-scare staging

presets.name: Golden Field Dusk
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Wide exterior dusk, Oklahoma wheat or scrub grassland, magic hour amber-gold raking light across flat terrain, sky going orange to cold blue above the horizon, figures small against an enormous sky reading as mythic outlaw-western silhouettes, the warmth of the light in direct aesthetic opposition to the nature of the figures, fast tungsten stock at magic hour pushing amber channel slightly warm, grain visible in sky gradient gradations, dusty desaturated earth tones in the landscape rather than lush green, horizon line dominant and low, the romantic western grammar in conscious service of dangerous content.

Group exterior night, the vampire family as American outlaw unit, vehicle headlights or distant fire as hard practical source creating rim and three-quarter effects across multiple faces, deep blacks consuming the spaces between figures, each face a separate pocket of light in overall darkness, dusty road or open field completely dissolved into background black, low angle making figures read as physically imposing mythic presences, grain heavy in the dark intervals between lit faces, amber-warm on lit skin, deep blue in shadow zones, group cohesion through shared darkness rather than controlled fill, no gothic costuming, no ornament, American road-family mythology.

presets.name: Oilfield Day Exterior
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Wide daytime exterior, Texas or Oklahoma oilfield or open flatland, harsh midday sun bleaching the sky toward white, parched and desaturated earth — pale brown, tan, grey-green scrub — no lush color anywhere in frame, hostile daylight as thematic inversion of night-danger, fast tungsten stock in daylight pushing toward cyan-blue in overexposed sky channel, earth tones robbed of saturation, wide empty establishing shot emphasizing displacement from normal American day-life, grain visible in sky gradients, overexposed sky edges eating into the landscape horizon, the American West as dangerous and indifferent in any light.

presets.name: Intimate Portrait
presets.text: Adam Greenberg, Near Dark 1987, 35mm spherical 1.85:1, Arriflex, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative [period-probable]. Tight close-up, interior or transitional space — car interior, bar booth, doorway — subject lit by a single practical source with strong directionality, half the face lost in deep photochemical shadow with no detail recovery, mixed color temperature when applicable: warm tungsten key one side, cool ambient blue on the shadow side, fast tungsten stock rendering skin texture with visible grain and physical three-dimensionality rather than digital smoothness, eyes catching the directional practical source, no beauty key, no hair light, no fill card, slight halation on the bright side of the face from the fast stock, the darkness earning the light.

layers.name: Camera Negative
layers.text: Processing: Camera negative register, Kodak 35mm tungsten negative circa 1986. Maximum latitude — shadow zones hold partially recoverable detail, highlight rolloff gradual rather than hard-clipping. Film grain structured: fine and tight in midtones, coarser and more irregular in deep shadows. Slight blue-green color crossover in deep blacks from the tungsten emulsion base. Fast tungsten stock in daylight exterior conditions pushes toward cyan-blue in the sky channel. Skin tones warm and dimensioned in tungsten-lit environments. This is the full information state before any photochemical timing choices commit the look.
layers.neg: no color grading, no timing, no sharpening, no noise reduction, no crushed blacks, no baked-in contrast, no digital clarity, no stylization

layers.name: Theatrical Release Print
layers.text: Processing: Theatrical release print, CFI lab printer-light timing, Technicolor print stock (Kodak 5384 period-probable), Hollywood 1987. Blacks deepen and lose shadow detail relative to the negative — the velvety quality is a print characteristic, not a negative one. Highlight structure compresses toward clipping at the extremes. The blue-night / amber-dawn polarity becomes more pronounced and photochemically committed. Each generational step — negative to interpositive to internegative to release print — adds its own grain layer; total grain is cumulative. This is the film as a 1987 cinema audience saw it: decisions made and irreversible.
layers.neg: no clean digital transfer, no modern restoration, no noise reduction, no sharpening, no HDR, no digital shadow recovery

layers.name: Telecine
layers.text: Processing: Telecine transfer, film-to-tape, BetaSP or 1" C-format probable, flying-spot or Rank Cintel, late 1980s CRT monitor color space. Black levels lift slightly compared to theatrical projection — some shadow detail partially recoverable versus the release print. Color reads cooler or flatter depending on transfer operator corrections. Scan sharpening introduces video-texture edge artifacts distinct from film grain. The grain pattern shifts character: more luminance-channel video noise, less structured photochemical grain. The velvety theatrical blacks become grayish-dark on period CRT monitors. This is the version that fed VHS duplication: pre-degraded, already technologically mediated.
layers.neg: no clean digital image, no modern transfer, no 4K restoration, no theatrical blacks, no sharp photochemical grain, no HDR

layers.name: Broadcast Transfer
layers.text: Processing: VHS duplication or broadcast transmission of the telecine master, late 1980s home video. Color information compressed; chroma bleeding slightly at high-saturation edges — neon signs, fire accents. Black levels variable by playback equipment. The nuance in the dusty desaturated palette collapses — careful differentiation between shades of pale earth flattens into undifferentiated midrange. Structured film grain replaced by random video noise in dark areas. Magic-hour burnouts clip to pure white more readily. Neon accent colors bloom with chroma fringing. The film's photographic sophistication is legible but stripped of subtlety — gritty 1980s horror VHS.
layers.neg: no clean image, no sharp detail, no modern transfer, no pure blacks, no digital clarity, no uncompressed color, no photochemical grain
